name: Danville Quarry
short_description: Historic quarry near Mineola producing quartz and feldspar specimens. Gentle terrain suitable for rockhounds exploring Missouri's Precambrian granite formations.
description:
Danville Quarry is a working or abandoned quarry operation in Montgomery County, Missouri that offers access to granitic material characteristic of the Ozark Precambrian basement rocks. The site sits at 712 feet elevation with gentle slopes, making it accessible for rockhounders interested in collecting quartz crystals, feldspar, and associated minerals. The quarry exposes Precambrian-age granitic rocks that were intruded roughly 1.4 billion years ago during the Proterozoic, providing excellent specimens of pegmatitic and granitic minerals.

The Ozark region is underlain by ancient granite and rhyolite that hosts commercial and amateur collecting opportunities. Danville Quarry's exposed faces and tailings present ideal conditions for finding well-formed quartz points, feldspar varieties, and quartz-feldspar intergrowths. The gentle topography and relatively accessible setting make this location suitable for family groups and beginners interested in examining granite mineralogy.

Before visiting, confirm current access status and ownership, as quarry operations and access policies can change. Bring appropriate tools for specimen collection and be prepared for variable ground conditions around old quarry operations. The site offers a chance to observe fresh granite exposures that few surface rockhounding locations in Missouri provide.
history: Danville Quarry represents exposure of Missouri's Precambrian granitic basement, formed during the Proterozoic eon approximately 1.4 billion years ago when massive granite plutons intruded the ancient continental crust. The Ozarks region has a long history of quarry operations extracting granite for building stone and aggregate, with Danville being one of several historic quarries that have attracted rockhounders seeking high-quality quartz and feldspar specimens from fresh exposures.
tips: Search the quarry tailings and base of exposed faces for loose quartz crystals and feldspar fragments - the coarser pegmatitic zones often yield larger, well-formed specimens. Bring a small hammer and chisel for carefully extracting crystals from granite walls, and wear safety glasses to protect from flying fragments. Look for milky white quartz points contrasting against pink feldspar matrix - these color contrasts make specimens easier to spot and indicate pegmatitic mineral concentrations.
terrain_notes: Elevation 712 feet with gentle slope. Rocky quarry terrain with exposed granite walls and loose stone fragments. Walking distance variable depending on quarry layout. Footing can be uneven around quarry features and old excavation areas. No established trails - access is cross-country over rubble and worked ground.
best_season: April - October. Spring through fall provides dry ground conditions ideal for quarry exploration. Summer heat is moderate for Missouri. Avoid winter when ground may be wet and slippery, and late fall/winter rain increases hazards around quarry edges.
written_directions: From Mineola, take local roads north toward Montgomery County interior (exact route depends on current access - verify before visiting). The quarry is located at approximately 38.90059, -91.541692 in the rural Montgomery County area. GPS coordinates are essential as the site is not on major highways. Confirm access road conditions and landowner permission before driving to the location.
road_conditions: Unknown - likely unpaved rural road. Confirm passability before visiting.
parking_notes: No formal parking area described. Park responsibly near quarry entrance if access is permitted.
collection_rules: Private property - permission from landowner required before collecting. Respect all no-trespassing signs and quarry safety barriers. No commercial collection. Avoid disturbing active quarry operations if site is still in use. All rockhounding regulations applying to private land in Missouri must be observed.
rules:
Permission from landowner required
No trespassing if posted
Respect quarry safety barriers
No commercial activity
Follow all local land-use restrictions
nearest_services: Mineola approximately 3-5 miles away. Verify fuel and supply availability before visiting.
accessibility_notes: Moderate difficulty terrain with uneven quarry ground and loose rock. No wheelchair access. Sturdy footwear required. Not suitable for visitors with limited mobility. Quarry edges and steep faces present fall hazards - stay back from unstable walls.

faq.question: What quartz and feldspar specimens can I find at Danville Quarry in Missouri?
faq.answer: Danville Quarry exposes Precambrian granite containing milky white quartz crystals, pink to white feldspar, and pegmatitic zones with larger, well-formed mineral specimens. The quarry's fresh faces provide access to better-preserved quartz points and feldspar intergrowths than most surface rockhounding sites in Missouri. Specimens range from small fragments to hand-sized collectibles depending on where crystallization occurred in the granite pluton.

faq.question: Is Danville Quarry open to rockhounders and do I need permission to collect?
faq.answer: Danville Quarry is private property, so you must obtain permission from the landowner before visiting or collecting. Access status and collecting rules may have changed, so confirm current conditions locally. Never trespass if no-trespassing signs are posted. Contact Montgomery County landowner records or local rockhounding clubs for current access information.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Danville Quarry?
faq.answer: Bring a small hammer, cold chisel, and pry bar for extracting specimens from granite faces. Safety glasses are essential to protect from rock fragments. A collection bag, notebook, and GPS unit help with navigation and specimen documentation. Sturdy boots with ankle support handle the uneven quarry terrain well. A headlamp or flashlight is useful if exploring shaded quarry areas.

faq.question: When is the best time to visit Danville Quarry for rockhounding?
faq.answer: April through October offers the best collecting conditions at Danville Quarry. Spring and early fall provide comfortable temperatures and dry ground. Summer is warm but manageable in Missouri. Avoid winter months when wet conditions make quarry footing hazardous and slippery. Late fall and spring rains increase danger around quarry edges, so plan visits after several dry days.

faq.question: Is Danville Quarry beginner-friendly for rockhounders new to collecting?
faq.answer: Yes, Danville Quarry suits beginner rockhounders interested in granite mineralogy. Quartz and feldspar are easy to identify, and the quarry's exposed faces clearly show mineral occurrence in granite. However, the moderate terrain with loose rock and quarry hazards requires caution and awareness. Families with children ages 8 and up can participate with supervision and proper safety precautions around unstable walls and loose fragments.

faq.question: How far is Danville Quarry from Mineola and what rockhounding opportunities exist nearby?
faq.answer: Danville Quarry is approximately 4 miles from Mineola in Montgomery County, Missouri. The surrounding Ozark region contains several granite exposures and historic quarries with similar Precambrian mineral specimens. Contact local rockhounding clubs or the Missouri Department of Natural Resources for information about other public or fee-access collecting sites in the Montgomery County area near Mineola.

meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and granite specimens at Danville Quarry near Mineola, Missouri. Private property with permission required. Precambrian granite exposures.
